attributes.stem: A 20-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department with an injured right leg. He was tackled heavily during a football match and was unable to get up because of the pain in his leg. An X-ray shows an uncomplicated fracture of the tibia. A backslab applied and the patient subsequently reviewed in the fracture clinic. A fibre-glass cast is applied and the patient is told that he will need to remain in the cast for up to six weeks.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate choice of VTE prophylaxis?
options: Low-molecular weight heparin for the duration of immobilisation
No pharmacological VTE prophylaxis is required
attributes.explanation:
Assessment of the risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE) and the need for VTE prophylaxis is an essential aspect of care in orthopaedic patients. Consideration of VTE prophylaxis consists of three main parts:

  1. Is pharmacological VTE prophylaxis indicated?

  2. Choice of anticoagulant

  3. Duration of prophylaxis

The decision as to whether prophylaxis is indicated is based on an assessment of VTE risk and an assessment of bleeding risk. VTE risk is both patient and procedure-dependent. Specific patient-dependent risk factors include previous VTE, malignancy, age >75, poor mobility prior to surgery, obesity and cardiovascular disease. Procedures carrying a high risk of VTE include hip and knee arthroplasty, hip fracture surgery as well as pelvic and multiple fracture surgery. Low-risk procedures include below the knee surgeries, upper extremity surgery and arthroscopies.

Regarding agent choice, low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) is the gold standard agent shown in randomised trials to be highly effective and associated with a low risk of major bleeding; LMWH should be avoided in patients with severe renal insufficiency. DOACs such as rivaroxaban, apixaban or dabigatran are being increasingly utilised, and have been shown to be as effective as LMWH in preventing VTE with no significant increase in bleeding. However, evidence only supports their use in total hip and knee arthroplasty. Fondaparinux has been shown to be more effective than LMWH in preventing VTE, however, is associated with an increased risk of bleeding and is therefore the second line choice. Also, like DOACs, it is only available in Australia to patients undergoing hip or knee replacement surgery or for patients with confirmed HITTS (Heparin Induced Thrombotic Thrombocytopaenia Syndrome).

For high risk procedures and patients, where the bleeding risk is low, pharmacologic prophylaxis is indicated for 10-14 days and may be discontinued once fully mobile and a 10-14 day course has been administered. For patients with a high bleeding risk, such as patients with active bleeding, intracranial haemorrhage, bleeding diathesis or coagulopathic patients, mechanical prophylaxis is indicated. Choices include intermittent pneumatic compression or graduated compression stockings.

For most low risk procedures and patients, no thromboprophylaxis is generally indicated other than early ambulation when feasible. This is based upon the POT-CAST trial as well as a number of other randomised trials and meta-analysises. However, there should be a low threshold for administering pharmacologic prophylaxis, particularly if there are additional risk factors present such as prior history of VTE or Achilles tendon rupture.

Therefore, for this patient, who has a low risk orthopaedic procedure and is young, with no known risk factors for VTE, no further prophylaxis is required other than early mobilisation.
learningPoints: For low-risk orthopaedic patients, VTE prophylaxis is not indicated, other than early mobilisation
LMWH is the first-line agent for VTE prophylaxis in high-risk orthopaedic patients
references: Lapidus LJ, Rosfors S, Ponzer S, Levander C, Elvin A, Lärfars G, de Bri E. Prolonged thromboprophylaxis with dalteparin after surgical treatment of achilles tendon rupture: a randomized, placebo-controlled study. J Orthop Trauma. 2007 Jan;21(1):52-7. doi: 10.1097/01.bot.0000250741.65003.14. PMID: 17211270. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17211270/

van Adrichem RA, Nemeth B, Algra A, le Cessie S, Rosendaal FR, Schipper IB, Nelissen RGHH, Cannegieter SC; POT-KAST and POT-CAST Group. Thromboprophylaxis after Knee Arthroscopy and Lower-Leg Casting. N Engl J Med. 2017 Feb 9;376(6):515-525. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa1613303. Epub 2016 Dec 3. PMID: 27959702. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27959702/

White RH, Gettner S, Newman JM, Trauner KB, Romano PS. Predictors of rehospitalization for symptomatic venous thromboembolism after total hip arthroplasty. N Engl J Med. 2000 Dec 14;343(24):1758-64. doi: 10.1056/NEJM200012143432403. PMID: 11114314. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11114314/
